---
title: "Stochastic Gradient Descent: Optimizing on a Budget"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Optimization"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# A consistent, restrained visual style for every figure in this article,
# matched to the rest of the Optimization track.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

CURVE   = "#2b3a67"    # the objective / full-batch path
STEP    = "#c1121f"    # the stochastic path / noise
ACCENT  = "#0a7d4d"    # the optimum / safe choices
GREY    = "#6c757d"
BATCH   = "#d97706"    # minibatch curves
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the first of the two **scaling** articles in
> the Optimization track's descent spine — the point where gradient descent is forced to
> confront datasets too large to touch all at once. It assumes [OPTM-06 *Gradient Descent*]
> for the update rule $\mathbf{x}_{k+1}=\mathbf{x}_k-\eta\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ and the
> fact that $-\nabla f$ is the steepest-descent direction, and it assumes [OPTM-07 *Step
> Size and Convergence*] for **$L$-smoothness**, the **descent lemma**, **strong
> convexity** with constant $\mu$, the **condition number** $\kappa=L/\mu$, and the
> baseline rates ($O(1/k)$ convex, linear $\rho^k$ strongly convex) that SGD is measured
> against.
>
> **What it borrows, and from where.** The single new analytic ingredient is
> probabilistic: the **expectation** of a random vector, the notion of an **unbiased
> estimator**, and the **variance of an average** of independent draws. These are owned by
> the *Probability Foundations* track; we restate exactly the three facts we use, inline,
> and derive everything we do with them. The optimization machinery — gradients, the
> descent lemma, strong convexity — is [OPTM-06]/[OPTM-07], used, not rebuilt.
>
> **What it sets up.** [OPTM-12 *Adaptive and Modern Optimizers*] (AdaGrad, RMSProp, Adam)
> are all refinements of the bare SGD step derived here. And SGD is the literal training
> engine that **Classification**, **Unsupervised Learning**, and every deep-model track
> link back to when they say "fit by SGD."

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

Gradient descent ([OPTM-06], [OPTM-07]) needs the *full* gradient at every step. But the
objectives the rest of the series minimizes are **finite sums** over data — a loss
averaged across $n$ examples — and when $n$ is in the millions, computing that full
gradient even once is ruinously expensive. This article derives the universal fix:
**stochastic gradient descent (SGD)**, which steps using the gradient of *one* example (or
a small **minibatch**) at a time. The central fact, derived from the definition of
expectation, is that a single example's gradient is an **unbiased estimator** of the full
gradient — *on average* it points the right way — so SGD is "noisy gradient descent" that
makes $n$ times more progress per unit of computation. We pay for that with **variance**:
the steps jitter, and we derive why this forces a *decaying* step size (the
Robbins–Monro conditions $\sum\eta_k=\infty$, $\sum\eta_k^2<\infty$) and why a *constant*
step instead converges only to a **noise floor** of size $O(\eta)$ around the optimum, never
into it. We prove the canonical SGD rates — $O(1/k)$ for strongly convex objectives,
$O(1/\sqrt{k})$ for merely convex ones — and locate the **minibatch** sweet spot as the
variance/cost trade-off it is. We sketch how **variance reduction** (SVRG/SAG) recovered
the fast linear rate the field thought SGD had forfeited, and we close at the honest edge:
the small- and medium-scale, ill-conditioned problems where full-batch GD or L-BFGS still
wins outright.

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
When the objective is an average over $n$ data points,
$f(\mathbf{x})=\tfrac1n\sum_{i=1}^n f_i(\mathbf{x})$, the full gradient
$\nabla f=\tfrac1n\sum_i\nabla f_i$ costs $n$ gradient evaluations per step. **Stochastic
gradient descent replaces it with the gradient of a single randomly chosen example**,
$$
\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \eta_k\,\nabla f_{i_k}(\mathbf{x}_k),
\qquad i_k \text{ drawn uniformly at random.}
$$
This is legitimate because $\nabla f_{i_k}$ is an **unbiased estimator** of $\nabla f$:
its expectation over the random draw *is* the full gradient, so on average SGD steps
downhill. It is $n$ times cheaper per step, but its steps are **noisy** — they scatter
around the true descent direction with a variance that does not vanish at the optimum. Two
consequences follow and define the method. **(1)** A *constant* step size cannot converge
to the exact minimum; it settles into a **noise floor** of radius $\propto\eta$ and bounces
there forever. To actually converge you must **decay the step size**, fast enough to kill
the noise ($\sum\eta_k^2<\infty$) but slowly enough to still travel ($\sum\eta_k=\infty$).
**(2)** The price of the noise is a *slower* rate: $O(1/k)$ for strongly convex problems
(versus GD's geometric $\rho^k$) and $O(1/\sqrt{k})$ for convex ones. **Minibatching** —
averaging $b$ examples per step — buys back accuracy by cutting the variance by $1/b$, at
$b\times$ the cost: the dial between cheap-and-noisy and expensive-and-clean.
:::

Everything below orbits that box. The update will look like a trivial edit of
[OPTM-06]'s — "use one example's gradient instead of all of them" — but that single change
trades a *deterministic descent guarantee* (the descent lemma forced $f$ down every step)
for a *statement in expectation*, and re-deriving the convergence theory under that
weakening is the whole technical content. The reward is the only thing that scales:
modern machine learning is trained by SGD because no full-batch method can afford to look
at the whole dataset for every one of millions of steps.

# Why we are here: the gradient itself is too expensive {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view for the entire article: **when even a single full gradient is
too expensive to compute, how do we keep descending — and what does it cost us to descend
on an estimate instead of the real thing?** Every derivation below answers a piece of
that.

## The finite-sum objective, and the cost wall {#sec-finite-sum}

Almost every objective the rest of the series minimizes has the same shape: a **loss
averaged over a dataset**. Least squares averages squared residuals; logistic regression
averages a log-loss; a neural network averages a cross-entropy. Write it generally as

$$
f(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i(\mathbf{x}),
$$ {#eq-finite-sum}

where $\mathbf{x}\in\mathbb{R}^p$ is the parameter vector we are optimizing and $f_i$ is the
loss contributed by the $i$-th data point (so $f_i$ depends on the parameters *and* on that
one example, which is fixed). The number $n$ is the **dataset size** and $p$ the **parameter
dimension**; in the regime that birthed SGD, $n$ is enormous.

Because the derivative of a sum is the sum of the derivatives (and the constant $\tfrac1n$
pulls out), the full gradient inherits the same average structure:

$$
\nabla f(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \nabla f_i(\mathbf{x}).
$$ {#eq-full-grad}

Here is the cost wall. Gradient descent ([OPTM-06]) needs $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ at
*every* iteration. By @eq-full-grad, computing it once means evaluating $n$ per-example
gradients $\nabla f_i$ and averaging them — an $O(n)$ pass over the *entire dataset*. And
[OPTM-07] told us GD typically needs *many* iterations (the count scales with the condition
number $\kappa$). So the total work is roughly $(\text{iterations})\times n$. When
$n=10^6$ examples and you want $10^3$ iterations, that is $10^9$ per-example gradient
evaluations — and a single full-batch step does not even *move* until all $10^6$ are summed.
For the datasets that defined modern machine learning, one full gradient can take minutes;
GD would take a step a minute and need thousands of them.

The waste is glaring once you say it aloud. To take *one* step, GD reads all $n$ examples,
forms a precise average, and then moves a single short distance downhill. If the $n$
examples are at all redundant — and real datasets are massively redundant, full of
near-duplicate examples — most of that precision is thrown away. **We computed a gradient
accurate to many digits and then took a small step.** Surely a *rough* estimate of the
direction, computed from a tiny fraction of the data, would point downhill well enough to
step — and then we could take $n$ such steps in the time GD takes one.

That is the entire idea of SGD, and the rest of the article makes "well enough" precise.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why is the gradient an average over data in the first place — couldn't the objective be
anything?"* In principle yes, but the objectives we actually minimize in statistics and ML
are **empirical risks**: an average of a per-example loss, $f(\mathbf{x})=\tfrac1n\sum_i
\ell(\mathbf{x};\text{data}_i)$, which is the sample stand-in for an *expected* loss
$\mathbb{E}_{\text{data}}[\ell(\mathbf{x};\text{data})]$ over the true data distribution.
The finite-sum form @eq-finite-sum is not a coincidence; it is what "fit a model to data
by minimizing average loss" *means*. That is also why SGD generalizes so cleanly to the
streaming/online setting: if you can draw fresh examples from the distribution, a single
example's gradient is an unbiased estimate of the gradient of the *population* risk, and
SGD optimizes the thing you actually care about (expected loss) without ever forming the
empirical sum. The interviewer is checking that you see "minimize average loss over data"
and "finite-sum optimization" as the same sentence.
:::

## A two-line history: from a 1951 statistics paper to every GPU {#sec-history}

SGD predates machine learning by decades. **Herbert Robbins and Sutton Monro**, in a 1951
paper titled *A Stochastic Approximation Method*, asked a purely statistical question: how
do you find the root of a function you can only observe through *noisy* measurements? Their
answer — step in the (noisy) measured direction, with a step size that *shrinks over time*
in a precisely calibrated way — is exactly SGD, and the step-size conditions we derive in
@sec-decay still bear their name. The method sat in the statistics literature as "stochastic
approximation" until the data deluge of the 2000s made full-batch gradients unaffordable,
at which point this 1951 idea turned out to be the *only* thing that scaled to millions of
examples and parameters. Every modern deep network is trained by Robbins and Monro's
procedure, dressed up with momentum ([OPTM-08]) and per-coordinate scaling ([OPTM-12]).
Holding this history in mind explains the article's shape: SGD is *gradient descent
married to the theory of estimating something from a noisy sample*, and the noise is not a
nuisance bolted on — it is the entire reason the method is cheap, and the entire source of
everything that makes it behave differently from GD.

# Intuition before symbols: a drunk who walks downhill on average {#sec-intuition}

Picture [OPTM-06]'s blindfolded walker on the hillside again, but now add a twist that
changes everything. The walker can no longer feel the true slope under their feet. Instead,
at each step they *poll a single, randomly chosen informant* — one data point — who shouts a
direction. Each informant is biased by their own example: one near-duplicate point insists
"steepest descent is *that* way," a different point shouts something a bit off. No single
informant gives the true downhill direction. **But the informants are unbiased: if you
averaged all $n$ of their answers you would get exactly the true slope.** So each one,
individually, is the true direction plus some zero-mean error.

The walker's strategy is reckless but effective: *don't wait to poll everyone — just step in
the direction the one random informant shouted, then poll a new one and step again.* Any
single step might go slightly sideways or even briefly uphill, because that informant was
off. But because the informants are right *on average*, the long-run drift is downhill — and
the walker covers $n$ steps in the time the careful full-gradient walker covers one. This is
the trade at the heart of SGD: **trade a precise step for many noisy ones.** @fig-drunk
sketches the two walks on the same bowl.

The picture also previews the two facts that will dominate the formal sections. First, **the
noise never fully goes away.** Even standing right at the bottom of the valley — where the
*true* slope is zero — each individual informant still shouts a nonzero direction (their own
example is not minimized by the global optimum). So a fixed-length step keeps getting kicked
around the minimum; it cannot settle. To actually come to rest, the walker must **take
shorter and shorter steps** as they go, so the late kicks are too small to matter. Second,
**polling a handful of informants and averaging their answers** (a minibatch) gives a less
noisy direction than polling one — the averaging cancels some of the disagreement — at the
cost of more polling per step.

```{python}
#| label: fig-drunk
#| fig-cap: "Full-batch gradient descent (navy) versus stochastic gradient descent (red) on the same quadratic bowl, started at the same point. GD computes the exact gradient and walks a smooth, direct path to the minimum (green). SGD uses one random example's gradient per step: its path is a jittery, drunken walk -- each step is only the true direction *plus zero-mean noise* -- yet it drifts unmistakably downhill and reaches the neighbourhood of the minimum in many more, but individually $n$-times-cheaper, steps. The jitter that never quite settles is the noise floor of @sec-noise-floor."
rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
# A finite-sum quadratic: f_i(x) = 1/2 (x - c_i)^T A (x - c_i), so the full
# minimizer is the centroid of the c_i; each f_i pulls toward its own c_i.
n = 60
A = np.array([[3.0, 0.4], [0.4, 1.0]])
centers = rng.normal(0, 1.0, size=(n, 2))
xstar = centers.mean(axis=0)            # exact minimizer of the average

def full_grad(x):
    return A @ (x - centers.mean(axis=0))
def stoch_grad(x, i):
    return A @ (x - centers[i])

x0 = np.array([2.6, 2.4])
# Full-batch GD
eta = 0.18
xg = x0.copy(); pathG = [xg.copy()]
for _ in range(40):
    xg = xg - eta * full_grad(xg); pathG.append(xg.copy())
pathG = np.array(pathG)
# SGD, constant step (to show the jitter / noise floor)
xs = x0.copy(); pathS = [xs.copy()]
for _ in range(220):
    i = rng.integers(n)
    xs = xs - 0.07 * stoch_grad(xs, i); pathS.append(xs.copy())
pathS = np.array(pathS)

gx = np.linspace(-2.0, 3.2, 200); gy = np.linspace(-2.0, 3.0, 200)
GX, GY = np.meshgrid(gx, gy)
Z = np.zeros_like(GX)
d = np.stack([GX - xstar[0], GY - xstar[1]], axis=-1)
Z = 0.5 * (d @ A * d).sum(axis=-1)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.6, 4.6))
ax.contour(GX, GY, Z, levels=18, colors=GREY, linewidths=0.7, alpha=0.7)
ax.plot(pathS[:, 0], pathS[:, 1], "-", color=STEP, lw=0.9, alpha=0.8, label="SGD (one example/step)")
ax.plot(pathG[:, 0], pathG[:, 1], "-o", color=CURVE, ms=3.5, lw=1.6, label="full-batch GD")
ax.scatter([x0[0]], [x0[1]], color="black", s=55, zorder=6, label="start")
ax.scatter([xstar[0]], [xstar[1]], color=ACCENT, s=110, zorder=6,
           edgecolor="white", linewidth=1.4, label="minimum")
ax.set_xlabel("$x_1$"); ax.set_ylabel("$x_2$")
ax.set_title("Smooth full-batch walk vs. drunken stochastic walk")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9)
ax.set_aspect("equal")
plt.tight_layout()
```

# The formal development {#sec-formal}

We now make the drunken walk precise. The whole theory rests on one probabilistic fact —
that the random per-example gradient is *unbiased* — and on tracking what its *variance*
does to the convergence machinery of [OPTM-07].

## The three facts we borrow from probability {#sec-prob-facts}

Just as [OPTM-06] restated the Taylor expansion at the level it used it, we restate here the
three probability facts SGD needs, no more. They are owned by the *Probability Foundations*
track; everything we *do* with them is derived in full below.

Let $\mathbf{g}$ be a **random vector** — a vector whose value depends on a random draw (for
us, which example we picked). Its **expectation** $\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{g}]$ is the
probability-weighted average of its possible values, taken coordinate by coordinate. We use
exactly three properties:

1. **Linearity of expectation.** For any (non-random) matrix or scalar $M$ and random
   vectors $\mathbf{g},\mathbf{h}$, $\mathbb{E}[M\mathbf{g}+\mathbf{h}]
   = M\,\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{g}]+\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{h}]$. The average of a linear combination
   is the linear combination of the averages. For a draw that is *uniform* over the indices
   $\{1,\dots,n\}$ — each picked with probability $\tfrac1n$ — the expectation of a function
   $h(i)$ of the index is just the plain average $\mathbb{E}_i[h(i)]=\tfrac1n\sum_{i=1}^n
   h(i)$.

2. **Unbiased estimator.** A random quantity $\widehat\theta$ is an **unbiased estimator**
   of a target $\theta$ if $\mathbb{E}[\widehat\theta]=\theta$ — its average value, over the
   randomness, hits the target exactly (it may be wrong on any single draw, but it is not
   *systematically* wrong).

3. **Variance of a vector, and of an average.** The (total) **variance** of $\mathbf{g}$ is
   $\operatorname{Var}(\mathbf{g})=\mathbb{E}\bigl[\|\mathbf{g}-\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{g}]\|^2\bigr]$
   — the expected squared distance of $\mathbf{g}$ from its own mean, a single nonnegative
   number measuring how much it scatters. The one structural fact we need: for the **average
   of $b$ independent draws** $\bar{\mathbf{g}}=\tfrac1b\sum_{j=1}^b\mathbf{g}_j$, each with
   variance $\sigma^2$, the variance of the average is $\sigma^2/b$. Averaging $b$
   independent estimates cuts the variance by a factor of $b$. (We re-derive this exact fact
   in @sec-minibatch, because it *is* the minibatch trade-off.)

::: {.callout-note title="What we borrowed, and where it is built"}
The expectation, linearity, the unbiased-estimator definition, and the variance-of-an-average
fact are constructed from the ground up in the **Probability Foundations** track. We import
only their statements. Everything optimization-specific — that the per-example gradient is
unbiased, what its variance does to the descent lemma, why the step size must decay — is
derived below from these three facts plus the [OPTM-07] machinery.
:::

## The stochastic gradient, and why it is unbiased {#sec-unbiased}

Define the SGD step. At iteration $k$, draw an index $i_k$ uniformly at random from
$\{1,\dots,n\}$ and step using *only* that example's gradient:

$$
\boxed{\;\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \eta_k\,\nabla f_{i_k}(\mathbf{x}_k).\;}
$$ {#eq-sgd}

Here $\nabla f_{i_k}(\mathbf{x}_k)$ is the **stochastic gradient** — one example's gradient,
standing in for the full average @eq-full-grad — and $\eta_k$ is the step size, written with
a subscript $k$ because, as we will prove, it generally must *change* over the iterations.
Compared to [OPTM-06]'s update the only edit is $\nabla f\mapsto\nabla f_{i_k}$: an $O(n)$
computation replaced by an $O(1)$ one.

The whole legitimacy of the method rests on one claim: *on average, the stochastic gradient
is the full gradient.* We prove it directly from the definition of expectation under a
uniform draw. Treat $\mathbf{x}_k$ as fixed (we are averaging only over the random index
$i_k$), and take the expectation of the stochastic gradient:

$$
\mathbb{E}_{i_k}\bigl[\nabla f_{i_k}(\mathbf{x}_k)\bigr]
= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Pr(i_k = i)\,\nabla f_i(\mathbf{x}_k)
= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{n}\,\nabla f_i(\mathbf{x}_k)
= \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\nabla f_i(\mathbf{x}_k).
$$

The first equality is the definition of expectation (sum over outcomes, weighted by their
probabilities); the second uses the uniform draw, $\Pr(i_k=i)=\tfrac1n$ for every $i$; the
third pulls the constant $\tfrac1n$ out of the sum. The right-hand side is *exactly* the
full gradient @eq-full-grad. So:

::: {#lem-unbiased}
**Lemma (the stochastic gradient is unbiased).** With $i_k$ drawn uniformly from
$\{1,\dots,n\}$, the stochastic gradient is an unbiased estimator of the full gradient:
$$
\mathbb{E}_{i_k}\bigl[\nabla f_{i_k}(\mathbf{x}_k)\bigr] = \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k).
$$ {#eq-unbiased}
:::

This is the linchpin. Write the stochastic gradient as the truth plus an error,
$$
\nabla f_{i_k}(\mathbf{x}_k) = \underbrace{\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)}_{\text{true direction}}
   + \underbrace{\bigl(\nabla f_{i_k}(\mathbf{x}_k) - \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)\bigr)}_{\text{noise }\boldsymbol\xi_k},
$$
and @eq-unbiased says precisely that the noise $\boldsymbol\xi_k$ has **mean zero**:
$\mathbb{E}_{i_k}[\boldsymbol\xi_k]=\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)-\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)=\mathbf{0}$.
SGD is therefore *gradient descent driven by the true gradient corrupted by zero-mean
noise.* The expected step is exactly the GD step; individual steps scatter around it. That
single sentence is what lets us salvage convergence — but only in expectation, and only at a
price set by how big the noise is.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Does the order in which you sweep through the data matter — random draws versus a fixed
cycle?"* It matters, and the distinction is worth getting right. Drawing $i_k$ **uniformly
at random with replacement** (the textbook assumption) is exactly what makes @eq-unbiased
hold cleanly, draw by draw — that is why the proofs use it. In practice, people instead do
**random reshuffling**: permute the dataset once per *epoch* (one pass through all $n$
examples) and sweep through the permutation, so every example is used exactly once per
epoch. Reshuffling is *not* unbiased draw-by-draw (later draws in an epoch are correlated
with earlier ones — you can't redraw an example you've used), so the clean per-step
analysis breaks; but it is empirically faster and is now known to converge at least as well.
A *fixed, never-shuffled* cycle is the dangerous case: it can bias the path if the data are
ordered (all class-0 examples first, then all class-1). The honest one-liner: *shuffle every
epoch; never feed sorted data in a fixed order.*
:::

## Variance: the price of the estimate, and the bounded-variance assumption {#sec-variance}

Unbiasedness says the noise is centered; it says nothing about how *large* the noise is, and
the size is what governs everything. Measure it by the variance of the stochastic gradient
about its mean — using @eq-unbiased, the mean *is* $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$:

$$
\operatorname{Var}_{i_k}\bigl(\nabla f_{i_k}(\mathbf{x}_k)\bigr)
= \mathbb{E}_{i_k}\bigl[\,\|\nabla f_{i_k}(\mathbf{x}_k) - \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)\|^2\,\bigr]
= \mathbb{E}_{i_k}\bigl[\|\boldsymbol\xi_k\|^2\bigr].
$$

The standard structural assumption that makes the theory go through is that this variance is
**bounded** — there is a finite ceiling $\sigma^2$ on how much the per-example gradients
disagree with their average:

$$
\mathbb{E}_{i_k}\bigl[\|\nabla f_{i_k}(\mathbf{x}_k) - \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)\|^2\bigr]
\;\le\; \sigma^2
\qquad\text{for all } \mathbf{x}_k.
$$ {#eq-bounded-var}

Read $\sigma^2$ as **how heterogeneous the dataset is**: if all $n$ examples were identical,
every $\nabla f_i$ would equal the average and $\sigma^2=0$ (SGD would be exact GD); the more
the examples disagree about which way is downhill, the larger $\sigma^2$. The crucial,
method-defining feature is the next sentence.

::: {.callout-important title="The fact that makes SGD different from GD"}
**The gradient noise does not vanish at the optimum.** At the true minimizer
$\mathbf{x}^\star$ the *full* gradient is zero, $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^\star)=\mathbf{0}$ — but
the individual per-example gradients $\nabla f_i(\mathbf{x}^\star)$ are generally **not**
zero (no single example is minimized exactly where the global average is). They merely
*cancel in the average*. So even sitting exactly at $\mathbf{x}^\star$, a stochastic step
$-\eta\nabla f_{i_k}(\mathbf{x}^\star)$ is a nonzero kick of size $\propto\eta\sigma$. SGD
*cannot stand still at the optimum* the way GD does (where $\nabla f=\mathbf{0}$ freezes the
iteration). This single fact is the source of the noise floor (@sec-noise-floor), the need
for a decaying step (@sec-decay), and the slower rate (@sec-rate).
:::

@fig-variance makes $\sigma^2$ visible: it shows, at three points of a finite-sum problem,
how widely the individual per-example gradients fan out around their true average.

```{python}
#| label: fig-variance
#| fig-cap: 'The gradient noise, made visible. For a finite-sum quadratic, at three locations (far from the optimum, near it, and *at* it), the thin grey arrows are individual per-example gradients $\nabla f_i$ and the thick red arrow is their average, the true gradient $\nabla f$. Far away (left) every example roughly agrees on ''downhill,'' so the noise is small relative to the signal. *At the optimum* (right) the true gradient is zero -- the red arrow vanishes -- yet the individual arrows are as long as ever and merely cancel. That non-vanishing scatter at the optimum is $\sigma^2$, and it is why a fixed-step SGD can never settle.'
rng = np.random.default_rng(3)
n = 24
A = np.array([[2.0, 0.0], [0.0, 2.0]])
centers = rng.normal(0, 1.3, size=(n, 2))
xstar = centers.mean(axis=0)
def gi(x, i): return A @ (x - centers[i])
def gfull(x): return A @ (x - xstar)

pts = {"far from optimum": xstar + np.array([2.6, 1.8]),
       "near optimum":     xstar + np.array([0.5, 0.3]),
       "at optimum":       xstar.copy()}
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(10.0, 3.5))
for ax, (name, x0) in zip(axes, pts.items()):
    for i in range(n):
        g = gi(x0, i)
        ax.arrow(x0[0], x0[1], -0.15 * g[0], -0.15 * g[1],
                 color=GREY, alpha=0.55, width=0.004, head_width=0.06, length_includes_head=True)
    gf = gfull(x0)
    ax.arrow(x0[0], x0[1], -0.15 * gf[0], -0.15 * gf[1],
             color=STEP, width=0.02, head_width=0.14, length_includes_head=True, zorder=5)
    ax.scatter([x0[0]], [x0[1]], color=CURVE, s=40, zorder=6)
    ax.scatter([xstar[0]], [xstar[1]], color=ACCENT, s=70, zorder=4, edgecolor="white")
    ax.set_title(name, fontsize=10)
    ax.set_xlim(xstar[0] - 2.0, xstar[0] + 3.6)
    ax.set_ylim(xstar[1] - 2.4, xstar[1] + 2.8)
    ax.set_aspect("equal")
    ax.set_xticks([]); ax.set_yticks([])
plt.tight_layout()
```

## One stochastic step, in expectation {#sec-one-step}

We now redo [OPTM-07]'s one-step analysis, but for the stochastic step, and watch the
variance enter. Recall the **descent lemma** ([OPTM-07], @sec-smoothness there): for an
$L$-smooth $f$ and any step $\mathbf{x}_{k+1}=\mathbf{x}_k+\mathbf{d}$,

$$
f(\mathbf{x}_{k+1}) \le f(\mathbf{x}_k) + \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^\top\mathbf{d}
   + \frac{L}{2}\|\mathbf{d}\|^2.
$$

This is a deterministic inequality; it holds for *any* step, including a stochastic one.
Plug in the SGD step $\mathbf{d}=-\eta\,\nabla f_{i_k}(\mathbf{x}_k)$, abbreviating
$\mathbf{g}_k=\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ (true gradient) and
$\mathbf{g}_k^{(i)}=\nabla f_{i_k}(\mathbf{x}_k)$ (stochastic gradient), with a *constant*
step $\eta$ for now:

$$
f(\mathbf{x}_{k+1}) \le f(\mathbf{x}_k)
   - \eta\,\mathbf{g}_k^\top\mathbf{g}_k^{(i)}
   + \frac{L\eta^2}{2}\|\mathbf{g}_k^{(i)}\|^2.
$$

This bound is *random* — it depends on which example $i_k$ was drawn. To extract something
usable, take the expectation over $i_k$ (with $\mathbf{x}_k$ held fixed) and use linearity of
expectation term by term:

$$
\mathbb{E}_{i_k}\bigl[f(\mathbf{x}_{k+1})\bigr] \le f(\mathbf{x}_k)
   - \eta\,\mathbf{g}_k^\top\,\mathbb{E}_{i_k}\bigl[\mathbf{g}_k^{(i)}\bigr]
   + \frac{L\eta^2}{2}\,\mathbb{E}_{i_k}\bigl[\|\mathbf{g}_k^{(i)}\|^2\bigr].
$$

Now substitute the two probability facts. The middle term uses unbiasedness @eq-unbiased,
$\mathbb{E}_{i_k}[\mathbf{g}_k^{(i)}]=\mathbf{g}_k$, so $\mathbf{g}_k^\top\mathbb{E}[\cdots]
=\mathbf{g}_k^\top\mathbf{g}_k=\|\mathbf{g}_k\|^2$. The last term needs the **expected
squared length** of the stochastic gradient; here is where variance enters, via the
identity (true for any random vector) that the mean of the squared length equals the squared
length of the mean plus the variance:

$$
\mathbb{E}_{i_k}\bigl[\|\mathbf{g}_k^{(i)}\|^2\bigr]
= \underbrace{\bigl\|\mathbb{E}_{i_k}[\mathbf{g}_k^{(i)}]\bigr\|^2}_{=\,\|\mathbf{g}_k\|^2}
  + \underbrace{\mathbb{E}_{i_k}\bigl[\|\mathbf{g}_k^{(i)} - \mathbf{g}_k\|^2\bigr]}_{\le\,\sigma^2}
\;\le\; \|\mathbf{g}_k\|^2 + \sigma^2.
$$

This is the vector form of "mean of squares = square of mean + variance," and it is short
enough to derive on the spot rather than cite. Write $\boldsymbol\mu=\mathbb{E}_{i_k}
[\mathbf{g}_k^{(i)}]=\mathbf{g}_k$ (the mean, equal to the true gradient by unbiasedness
@eq-unbiased), add and subtract it inside the squared norm, and expand using
$\|\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b}\|^2=\|\mathbf{a}\|^2+2\mathbf{a}^\top\mathbf{b}+\|\mathbf{b}\|^2$:

$$
\mathbb{E}_{i_k}\bigl[\|\mathbf{g}_k^{(i)}\|^2\bigr]
= \mathbb{E}_{i_k}\bigl[\|(\mathbf{g}_k^{(i)}-\boldsymbol\mu)+\boldsymbol\mu\|^2\bigr]
= \mathbb{E}_{i_k}\bigl[\|\mathbf{g}_k^{(i)}-\boldsymbol\mu\|^2\bigr]
  + 2\,\mathbb{E}_{i_k}\bigl[(\mathbf{g}_k^{(i)}-\boldsymbol\mu)\bigr]^\top\boldsymbol\mu
  + \|\boldsymbol\mu\|^2.
$$

The cross term vanishes because $\boldsymbol\mu$ is non-random (pull it out of the
expectation) and $\mathbb{E}_{i_k}[\mathbf{g}_k^{(i)}-\boldsymbol\mu]=\boldsymbol\mu-
\boldsymbol\mu=\mathbf{0}$. That leaves the variance $\mathbb{E}_{i_k}[\|\mathbf{g}_k^{(i)}-
\boldsymbol\mu\|^2]\le\sigma^2$ (the bounded-variance assumption @eq-bounded-var) plus
$\|\boldsymbol\mu\|^2=\|\mathbf{g}_k\|^2$, which is exactly the displayed bound. Substituting
both pieces back into the expected one-step inequality:

$$
\mathbb{E}_{i_k}\bigl[f(\mathbf{x}_{k+1})\bigr]
\le f(\mathbf{x}_k) - \eta\|\mathbf{g}_k\|^2
   + \frac{L\eta^2}{2}\bigl(\|\mathbf{g}_k\|^2 + \sigma^2\bigr)
= f(\mathbf{x}_k) - \eta\Bigl(1 - \frac{L\eta}{2}\Bigr)\|\mathbf{g}_k\|^2
   + \frac{L\eta^2}{2}\sigma^2.
$$ {#eq-sgd-onestep}

Compare @eq-sgd-onestep to [OPTM-07]'s deterministic one-step decrease
$f(\mathbf{x}_{k+1})\le f(\mathbf{x}_k)-\eta(1-\tfrac{L\eta}{2})\|\mathbf{g}_k\|^2$. **They
are identical except for one new term: $+\tfrac{L\eta^2}{2}\sigma^2$.** That term is the
entire difference between GD and SGD, and reading it tells the whole story:

- It is **positive** — it works *against* the descent. The GD part $-\eta(1-\tfrac{L\eta}{2})
  \|\mathbf{g}_k\|^2$ still pushes $f$ down, but the noise term pushes back up.
- It is proportional to $\sigma^2$ (more dataset heterogeneity $\Rightarrow$ more
  interference) and to $\eta^2$ (the noise hurts *quadratically* in the step size, while the
  descent helps only *linearly*).
- Crucially, it **does not depend on $\|\mathbf{g}_k\|$**. Near the optimum the true gradient
  $\mathbf{g}_k\to\mathbf{0}$, so the helpful descent term vanishes — but the harmful noise
  term $\tfrac{L\eta^2}{2}\sigma^2$ stays put. Close to the bottom, *noise dominates*, and
  the expected objective can no longer be guaranteed to decrease. This is the algebraic form
  of "SGD can't settle at the optimum."

@eq-sgd-onestep is the master inequality; both the noise floor and the convergence rate fall
out of it.

# Why a constant step cannot converge: the noise floor {#sec-noise-floor}

Suppose we run SGD with a *constant* step $\eta$, as in @fig-drunk. The master inequality
@eq-sgd-onestep already tells us this cannot converge to $\mathbf{x}^\star$, and we can pin
down exactly where it stalls. The expected one-step change is

$$
\mathbb{E}_{i_k}[f(\mathbf{x}_{k+1})] - f(\mathbf{x}_k)
\le -\eta\Bigl(1-\frac{L\eta}{2}\Bigr)\|\mathbf{g}_k\|^2 + \frac{L\eta^2}{2}\sigma^2.
$$

Take $\eta\le 1/L$ so the bracket $1-\tfrac{L\eta}{2}\ge\tfrac12$ (the safe regime from
[OPTM-07]). The change is negative — descent continues — only while the (good) first term
beats the (bad) second:

$$
\frac{\eta}{2}\|\mathbf{g}_k\|^2 \;>\; \frac{L\eta^2}{2}\sigma^2
\qquad\Longleftrightarrow\qquad
\|\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)\|^2 \;>\; L\eta\,\sigma^2.
$$

So SGD keeps making expected progress *as long as the true gradient is large enough* —
specifically while $\|\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)\|^2 > L\eta\sigma^2$. Once the iterate gets close
enough to the optimum that the true gradient shrinks below $\sqrt{L\eta}\,\sigma$, the noise
term wins and the expected objective stops decreasing. The iterates then bounce around a
neighbourhood of $\mathbf{x}^\star$ — the **noise floor** — whose size is set by the
threshold:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{floor radius (in gradient norm)} \;\sim\; \sqrt{L\eta}\,\sigma
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad \text{floor in } f \;\sim\; O(\eta\sigma^2).\;}
$$ {#eq-floor}

The objective settles at roughly $f^\star + O(\eta\sigma^2)$ above the true minimum. Two
practitioner facts follow immediately, and they are the daily reality of training:

- **Smaller $\eta$ $\Rightarrow$ lower floor, slower descent.** Halving $\eta$ halves the
  floor (the iterate ends up closer to $\mathbf{x}^\star$) but also roughly halves the
  per-step progress (slower to get there). This is the fundamental SGD tension, and it is why
  there is no single best constant step.
- **The loss curve plateaus, it does not converge.** A constant-step SGD training run shows a
  rapid early decrease followed by a noisy plateau — the loss rattling around the floor, not
  a clean approach to a minimum. Seeing that plateau and concluding "training is done" is a
  classic mistake; the fix is to *lower the step size*, which drops the floor and lets
  training resume.

@fig-noise-floor shows the plateau directly and confirms it scales with $\eta$.

```{python}
#| label: fig-noise-floor
#| fig-cap: 'The noise floor, measured. SGD with a *constant* step on a finite-sum quadratic, for three step sizes, plotting suboptimality $f(\mathbf{x}_k)-f^\star$ (log scale, averaged over runs to show the trend). Each run drops fast, then **plateaus** at a floor it never gets below -- and the floor height scales with $\eta$ exactly as @eq-floor predicts ($\eta=0.05$ floors ten times higher than $\eta=0.005$). A constant step buys speed *or* accuracy, never both. The dashed lines mark the predicted $O(\eta)$ floor levels.'
rng = np.random.default_rng(7)
n, p = 500, 10
B = rng.standard_normal((n, p))
xtrue = rng.standard_normal(p)
# f_i(x) = 1/2 (b_i^T x - t_i)^2 ; average is a least-squares quadratic.
t = B @ xtrue + 0.5 * rng.standard_normal(n)
# Exact minimizer and f* via normal equations.
xstar = np.linalg.solve(B.T @ B, B.T @ t)
def ffull(x): return 0.5 * np.mean((B @ x - t) ** 2)
fstar = ffull(xstar)
def gi(x, i): return (B[i] @ x - t[i]) * B[i]

def run_const(eta, steps=6000, reps=6):
    curves = []
    for _ in range(reps):
        x = np.zeros(p); hist = []
        for k in range(steps):
            i = rng.integers(n)
            x = x - eta * gi(x, i)
            if k % 20 == 0:
                hist.append(ffull(x) - fstar)
        curves.append(hist)
    return np.mean(np.array(curves), axis=0)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.8, 4.2))
ks = np.arange(0, 6000, 20)
for eta, col in [(0.05, STEP), (0.015, BATCH), (0.005, CURVE)]:
    gap = np.clip(run_const(eta), 1e-12, None)
    ax.semilogy(ks, gap, color=col, label=fr"$\eta={eta}$")
    ax.axhline(gap[-200:].mean(), color=col, ls=":", lw=1.0, alpha=0.7)
ax.set_xlabel("iteration $k$"); ax.set_ylabel(r"$f(\mathbf{x}_k)-f^\star$ (log scale)")
ax.set_title("Constant-step SGD plateaus at an $O(\\eta)$ noise floor")
ax.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9, title="constant step")
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**A constant learning rate cannot find the minimum of a finite-sum objective with SGD — by
design, not by bug.** The floor @eq-floor is not numerical error or an artifact of too few
iterations; it is a structural feature of stepping on a noisy estimate that does not vanish
at the optimum (@sec-variance). Running constant-step SGD for ten times as long does not get
you below the floor — it just keeps bouncing. The two honest fixes are (1) **decay the step
size** (@sec-decay), which lowers the floor toward zero over time, or (2) **increase the
batch size** (@sec-minibatch), which lowers $\sigma^2$ and hence the floor. Many real
training recipes do both. If a loss curve flattens far above where you know the optimum is,
the question is never "more iterations?" — it is "lower the step, or grow the batch."
:::

# How to actually converge: the decaying step size {#sec-decay}

The noise floor @eq-floor is proportional to $\eta$. The cure is therefore obvious in
outline: **shrink $\eta$ toward zero as the iterations proceed**, so the floor descends to
zero and the iterates can be squeezed onto $\mathbf{x}^\star$. But there is a tension. If we
shrink $\eta_k$ *too fast*, the steps become negligibly small before the iterate has had time
to *travel* to the optimum, and it stalls partway, frozen by tininess rather than by having
arrived. So $\eta_k$ must shrink **fast enough to kill the noise** but **slowly enough to
still cover unbounded distance**. Robbins and Monro (1951) made this precise.

::: {#thm-robbins-monro}
**The Robbins–Monro conditions.** A diminishing step-size schedule $\{\eta_k\}$ with
$\eta_k>0$ drives SGD to the optimum (in expectation, for a strongly convex smooth $f$) if

$$
\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\eta_k = \infty
\qquad\text{(steps sum to infinity: can travel any distance),}
$$ {#eq-rm1}
$$
\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\eta_k^2 < \infty
\qquad\text{(squared steps are summable: noise is damped).}
$$ {#eq-rm2}
:::

The two conditions encode exactly the two halves of the tension, and we can *read* each one
off the master inequality @eq-sgd-onestep — this is why they take the form they do, not as a
mysterious pair of requirements.

**Why $\sum\eta_k=\infty$ (the travel condition).** Summing the *good* descent term across
iterations, the total progress SGD can make scales like $\sum_k\eta_k\|\mathbf{g}_k\|^2$. To
move the iterate an unbounded distance — to reach an optimum that might start far away — the
cumulative step length $\sum_k\eta_k$ must be able to grow without bound. If the steps were
summable ($\sum\eta_k<\infty$, e.g. $\eta_k=1/k^2$), the iterate could travel at most a
*finite* total distance and would freeze short of any optimum farther away than that
distance. **Divergent sum = the walker can get anywhere.**

**Why $\sum\eta_k^2<\infty$ (the damping condition).** Summing the *bad* noise term
$\tfrac{L\eta_k^2}{2}\sigma^2$ across iterations, the total damage the noise inflicts on the
objective scales like $\sigma^2\sum_k\eta_k^2$. For that accumulated noise to remain *finite*
— so it does not swamp the descent over an infinite run — we need $\sum_k\eta_k^2<\infty$.
**Convergent sum of squares = the total noise injected is bounded**, so the floor @eq-floor,
now $\propto\eta_k$, is squeezed to zero.

The canonical schedule satisfying both is the **harmonic** $\eta_k = \eta_0/k$ (or
$\eta_0/(k+1)$): the harmonic series $\sum 1/k$ diverges (travel), while $\sum 1/k^2 =
\pi^2/6$ converges (damping). A gentler, very common choice is $\eta_k=\eta_0/\sqrt{k}$,
which satisfies @eq-rm1 ($\sum 1/\sqrt{k}=\infty$) but *not* @eq-rm2 ($\sum 1/k=\infty$) — it
does not formally guarantee convergence to the exact optimum, but converges to a *small*
floor and is the right schedule for the merely-convex $O(1/\sqrt k)$ rate (@sec-rate). Most
practical deep-learning schedules (step decay, cosine annealing, linear warmup-then-decay)
are engineering variations on "start larger to travel, end smaller to settle."

@fig-decay shows a decaying schedule threading the needle: it reaches the optimum where the
constant steps of @fig-noise-floor only plateaued.

```{python}
#| label: fig-decay
#| fig-cap: 'Decaying the step size beats the floor. The same finite-sum problem as @fig-noise-floor. **Grey/orange:** two *constant* steps, each plateauing at its own noise floor (@eq-floor). **Green:** the harmonic schedule $\eta_k=\eta_0/(k+1)$ satisfying the Robbins--Monro conditions @eq-rm1--@eq-rm2 -- it keeps descending past every constant-step floor, driving suboptimality toward zero (the slope is the $O(1/k)$ rate of @sec-rate). The decay is slow enough to keep travelling, fast enough to damp the noise.'
rng = np.random.default_rng(11)
n, p = 500, 10
B = rng.standard_normal((n, p)); xtrue = rng.standard_normal(p)
t = B @ xtrue + 0.5 * rng.standard_normal(n)
xstar = np.linalg.solve(B.T @ B, B.T @ t)
def ffull(x): return 0.5 * np.mean((B @ x - t) ** 2)
fstar = ffull(xstar)
def gi(x, i): return (B[i] @ x - t[i]) * B[i]

def run(schedule, steps=12000, reps=6):
    curves = []
    for _ in range(reps):
        x = np.zeros(p); hist = []
        for k in range(steps):
            eta = schedule(k)
            i = rng.integers(n)
            x = x - eta * gi(x, i)
            if k % 40 == 0:
                hist.append(ffull(x) - fstar)
        curves.append(hist)
    return np.mean(np.array(curves), axis=0)

ks = np.arange(0, 12000, 40)
const_big = run(lambda k: 0.03)
const_sm  = run(lambda k: 0.008)
decay     = run(lambda k: 0.25 / (k + 50))

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.8, 4.2))
ax.semilogy(ks, np.clip(const_big, 1e-12, None), color=BATCH, label=r"constant $\eta=0.03$")
ax.semilogy(ks, np.clip(const_sm, 1e-12, None), color=GREY, label=r"constant $\eta=0.008$")
ax.semilogy(ks, np.clip(decay, 1e-12, None), color=ACCENT, lw=2.3, label=r"decaying $\eta_k=0.25/(k+50)$")
ax.set_xlabel("iteration $k$"); ax.set_ylabel(r"$f(\mathbf{x}_k)-f^\star$ (log scale)")
ax.set_title("Robbins--Monro decay descends past every constant floor")
ax.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why not just use the decaying schedule from the start and forget constant steps?"* Two
reasons it is not that simple. **First, tuning $\eta_0$ is delicate**: too large and the
early steps overshoot before the decay catches up; too small and the schedule starves before
reaching the optimum (the travel condition is satisfied only *asymptotically*, and a tiny
$\eta_0$ makes the early sum grow painfully slowly). **Second, in deep learning the
strongly-convex assumptions behind the clean rate fail**, and a pure $1/k$ decay often decays
*too aggressively* — the step shrinks faster than the loss landscape warrants, and training
stalls. That is why practical recipes are hybrids: a constant (or warming-up) rate for a long
initial phase to make fast bulk progress, then *step decays* (cut $\eta$ by $10\times$ at
fixed milestones) to drop the floor in stages. The theory says "decay to converge"; the craft
is *when and how fast*, and it is genuinely problem-dependent.
:::

# Minibatching: buying back accuracy with the variance-of-an-average {#sec-minibatch}

Between "one example per step" (cheap, noisy) and "all $n$ per step" (expensive, exact) lies
the dial everyone actually turns: the **minibatch**. Pick a batch $\mathcal{B}_k$ of $b$
indices (uniformly, without replacement within the batch) and step on their *average*
gradient:

$$
\boxed{\;\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \eta_k\,\frac{1}{b}\sum_{i\in\mathcal{B}_k}\nabla f_i(\mathbf{x}_k).\;}
$$ {#eq-minibatch}

Call the batch gradient $\widehat{\mathbf{g}}_k=\tfrac1b\sum_{i\in\mathcal{B}_k}\nabla
f_i(\mathbf{x}_k)$. It is *still unbiased* — the average of unbiased estimators is unbiased,
since by linearity $\mathbb{E}[\widehat{\mathbf{g}}_k]=\tfrac1b\sum_{i\in\mathcal{B}_k}
\mathbb{E}[\nabla f_i]=\tfrac1b\cdot b\cdot\nabla f=\nabla f$ — so everything in @sec-unbiased
through @sec-decay applies verbatim with $\nabla f_{i_k}$ replaced by $\widehat{\mathbf{g}}_k$.
What *changes*, and changes favourably, is the variance. We derive the variance reduction
explicitly, because it *is* the entire value proposition of minibatching.

Treat the $b$ batch members as independent draws of a single-example gradient (the
with-replacement idealization; without-replacement is slightly better, by a finite-population
factor we note below). Each draw $\nabla f_i$ has the same variance $\sigma^2$ about the mean
$\nabla f$, by @eq-bounded-var. The variance of their average is, expanding the squared norm
and using that distinct draws are independent (so their cross-terms vanish in expectation):

$$
\operatorname{Var}(\widehat{\mathbf{g}}_k)
= \mathbb{E}\Bigl[\bigl\|\tfrac1b\textstyle\sum_{j=1}^b (\nabla f_{i_j} - \nabla f)\bigr\|^2\Bigr]
= \frac{1}{b^2}\,\mathbb{E}\Bigl[\bigl\|\textstyle\sum_{j=1}^b \boldsymbol\xi_j\bigr\|^2\Bigr],
$$

where $\boldsymbol\xi_j=\nabla f_{i_j}-\nabla f$ is the $j$-th draw's zero-mean noise.
Expand the squared norm of the sum into diagonal ($j=\ell$) and off-diagonal ($j\ne\ell$)
pieces:

$$
\mathbb{E}\Bigl[\bigl\|\textstyle\sum_j\boldsymbol\xi_j\bigr\|^2\Bigr]
= \sum_{j=1}^b\mathbb{E}\bigl[\|\boldsymbol\xi_j\|^2\bigr]
  + \sum_{j\ne\ell}\mathbb{E}\bigl[\boldsymbol\xi_j^\top\boldsymbol\xi_\ell\bigr].
$$

For the off-diagonal terms, independence and zero mean give
$\mathbb{E}[\boldsymbol\xi_j^\top\boldsymbol\xi_\ell]
=\mathbb{E}[\boldsymbol\xi_j]^\top\mathbb{E}[\boldsymbol\xi_\ell]=\mathbf{0}^\top\mathbf{0}=0$
(the expectation of a product of independent quantities is the product of expectations, and
each expectation is zero). So *every cross-term vanishes*, leaving only the $b$ diagonal
terms, each $\le\sigma^2$:

$$
\operatorname{Var}(\widehat{\mathbf{g}}_k)
= \frac{1}{b^2}\sum_{j=1}^b\mathbb{E}\bigl[\|\boldsymbol\xi_j\|^2\bigr]
\;\le\; \frac{1}{b^2}\cdot b\,\sigma^2
= \frac{\sigma^2}{b}.
$$ {#eq-batch-var}

::: {#lem-batch-var}
**Lemma (minibatch variance reduction).** A minibatch of size $b$ has gradient variance
$\sigma^2/b$ — the single-example variance divided by the batch size. Doubling the batch
halves the gradient noise (and quarters the noise *floor*, since the floor scales with
$\sigma^2$). (Without replacement, the variance is the slightly smaller
$\tfrac{\sigma^2}{b}\cdot\tfrac{n-b}{n-1}$, the *finite-population correction*; for $b\ll n$
this is essentially $\sigma^2/b$.)
:::

This is the whole minibatch trade-off, and it is governed by the $\sqrt{\cdot}$ that the
square-root in the standard deviation always brings:

- **Cost** of a batch step is $b\times$ a single-example step (you compute $b$ gradients).
- **Noise** (standard deviation) falls only as $\sigma/\sqrt{b}$ — the *square root* of the
  cost. To halve the gradient noise you must *quadruple* the batch and hence the cost.

So there are **diminishing returns to large batches**: going from $b=1$ to $b=4$ halves the
noise for $4\times$ the work — often worth it; going from $b=256$ to $b=1024$ also only halves
the noise, for another $4\times$ the work, by which point the variance may already be small
enough that you are just paying for precision you do not need. The sweet spot is usually a
*modest* batch (tens to a few hundred), large enough to give a usefully steady direction and
to saturate hardware parallelism (a GPU computes $b$ gradients almost as fast as one), small
enough that you still take many cheap, frequent steps. @fig-minibatch shows the variance
falling as $1/b$ and the resulting smoother descent.

```{python}
#| label: fig-minibatch
#| fig-cap: 'Minibatching trades noise for cost. **Left:** measured variance of the minibatch gradient versus batch size $b$ on a finite-sum problem (log--log); it falls as $\sigma^2/b$ (the dashed reference line has slope $-1$), confirming @eq-batch-var. **Right:** SGD descent for $b\in\{1,8,64\}$ at matched *cost* (so larger batches take proportionally fewer steps). Larger batches give a smoother, lower-floor path per step, but because each step costs $b\times$ more, the per-computation advantage has diminishing returns -- the $b=8$ and $b=64$ curves are close, while both beat the very jittery $b=1$.'
rng = np.random.default_rng(5)
n, p = 800, 12
B = rng.standard_normal((n, p)); xtrue = rng.standard_normal(p)
t = B @ xtrue + 0.5 * rng.standard_normal(n)
xstar = np.linalg.solve(B.T @ B, B.T @ t)
def ffull(x): return 0.5 * np.mean((B @ x - t) ** 2)
fstar = ffull(xstar)
allg = lambda x: (B @ x - t)[:, None] * B          # per-example gradients at x

# Left: empirical variance of the batch gradient vs b, at a fixed point.
x_probe = np.zeros(p)
G = allg(x_probe)                                  # n x p
gfull = G.mean(axis=0)
bs = np.array([1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128])
var_emp = []
for b in bs:
    samp = np.array([G[rng.choice(n, b, replace=False)].mean(axis=0) for _ in range(2000)])
    var_emp.append(np.mean(np.sum((samp - gfull) ** 2, axis=1)))
var_emp = np.array(var_emp)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.4, 4.0))
axL.loglog(bs, var_emp, "-o", color=STEP, ms=5, label="measured batch variance")
axL.loglog(bs, var_emp[0] / bs, "--", color=GREY, label=r"$\sigma^2/b$ (slope $-1$)")
axL.set_xlabel("batch size $b$"); axL.set_ylabel("variance of batch gradient")
axL.set_title("Variance falls as $1/b$")
axL.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)

# Right: matched-cost descent for several b.
def run_batch(b, total_grads=40000, eta=0.02, reps=5):
    steps = total_grads // b
    curves = []
    for _ in range(reps):
        x = np.zeros(p); hist = []
        for k in range(steps):
            idx = rng.choice(n, b, replace=False)
            g = (B[idx] @ x - t[idx]) @ B[idx] / b
            x = x - eta * g
            hist.append((k * b, ffull(x) - fstar))
        curves.append(np.array(hist))
    m = min(len(c) for c in curves)
    arr = np.mean([c[:m] for c in curves], axis=0)
    return arr[:, 0], arr[:, 1]

for b, col in [(1, STEP), (8, BATCH), (64, CURVE)]:
    xc, gap = run_batch(b)
    axR.semilogy(xc, np.clip(gap, 1e-10, None), color=col, label=fr"$b={b}$")
axR.set_xlabel("gradients computed (matched cost)")
axR.set_ylabel(r"$f-f^\star$ (log scale)")
axR.set_title("Matched-cost descent: diminishing returns")
axR.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

# The convergence rate: how much the noise costs {#sec-rate}

We can now state what SGD's noise costs in *speed*, and contrast it with the clean rates of
[OPTM-07]. The derivations parallel [OPTM-07]'s exactly, with the extra noise term from
@eq-sgd-onestep carried along, so we give the strongly-convex result in full and quote the
convex one.

## The strongly-convex rate: $O(1/k)$, not $\rho^k$ {#sec-strong-rate}

::: {#thm-sgd-rate}
**Theorem (SGD rate, strongly convex + smooth, decaying step).** Let $f$ be $\mu$-strongly
convex and $L$-smooth, with bounded gradient variance $\sigma^2$ (@eq-bounded-var). Run SGD
@eq-sgd with the harmonic step $\eta_k = \tfrac{2}{\mu(k+1)}$. Then the expected
suboptimality decays as
$$
\mathbb{E}\bigl[f(\mathbf{x}_k)\bigr] - f^\star \;\le\; \frac{C}{k}
\qquad\text{for a constant } C = O\!\Bigl(\frac{L\sigma^2}{\mu^2}\Bigr).
$$ {#eq-sgd-rate}
:::

::: {.proof}
We track the expected squared distance $D_k=\mathbb{E}\|\mathbf{x}_k-\mathbf{x}^\star\|^2$
and show it falls like $1/k$; converting to function value follows from smoothness. Expand
the squared distance after one step, exactly as in [OPTM-07]'s convex-rate proof but with
the stochastic gradient $\mathbf{g}_k^{(i)}$, and abbreviate $\mathbf{g}_k=\nabla
f(\mathbf{x}_k)$:

$$
\|\mathbf{x}_{k+1}-\mathbf{x}^\star\|^2
= \|\mathbf{x}_k - \eta_k\mathbf{g}_k^{(i)} - \mathbf{x}^\star\|^2
= \|\mathbf{x}_k-\mathbf{x}^\star\|^2
   - 2\eta_k\,(\mathbf{g}_k^{(i)})^\top(\mathbf{x}_k-\mathbf{x}^\star)
   + \eta_k^2\|\mathbf{g}_k^{(i)}\|^2.
$$

Take expectation over the draw $i_k$ (conditioning on $\mathbf{x}_k$). The cross term uses
unbiasedness @eq-unbiased to replace $\mathbf{g}_k^{(i)}$ by $\mathbf{g}_k$; the last term
uses the expected-squared-length bound $\mathbb{E}\|\mathbf{g}_k^{(i)}\|^2\le\|\mathbf{g}_k\|^2
+\sigma^2$ from @sec-one-step:

$$
\mathbb{E}_{i_k}\|\mathbf{x}_{k+1}-\mathbf{x}^\star\|^2
\le \|\mathbf{x}_k-\mathbf{x}^\star\|^2
   - 2\eta_k\,\mathbf{g}_k^\top(\mathbf{x}_k-\mathbf{x}^\star)
   + \eta_k^2\bigl(\|\mathbf{g}_k\|^2 + \sigma^2\bigr).
$$

Now use the [OPTM-07] machinery to control the cross term $-2\eta_k\,\mathbf{g}_k^\top
(\mathbf{x}_k-\mathbf{x}^\star)$ and absorb the unwanted $\eta_k^2\|\mathbf{g}_k\|^2$. We need
two inequalities from [OPTM-07], both applied between $\mathbf{x}_k$ and the optimum
$\mathbf{x}^\star$ (where $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^\star)=\mathbf{0}$ and $f(\mathbf{x}^\star)=
f^\star$):

- **Strong convexity** ([OPTM-07], @eq-strong there):
  $$
  \mathbf{g}_k^\top(\mathbf{x}_k-\mathbf{x}^\star)
  \;\ge\; f(\mathbf{x}_k)-f^\star + \tfrac{\mu}{2}\|\mathbf{x}_k-\mathbf{x}^\star\|^2.
  \tag{A}
  $$
  (This is the first-order strong-convexity inequality $f(\mathbf{x}^\star)\ge f(\mathbf{x}_k)
  +\mathbf{g}_k^\top(\mathbf{x}^\star-\mathbf{x}_k)+\tfrac\mu2\|\mathbf{x}^\star-\mathbf{x}_k\|^2$
  from [OPTM-07], rearranged.)
- **Smoothness as a gradient–suboptimality bound** ([OPTM-07], the descent lemma minimized
  over the step gives the "co-coercivity at the optimum" form):
  $$
  \tfrac{1}{2L}\|\mathbf{g}_k\|^2 \;\le\; f(\mathbf{x}_k)-f^\star.
  \tag{B}
  $$
  (For an $L$-smooth $f$, $f^\star=\min_{\mathbf y}f(\mathbf y)\le f\bigl(\mathbf{x}_k-
  \tfrac1L\mathbf{g}_k\bigr)\le f(\mathbf{x}_k)-\tfrac1{2L}\|\mathbf{g}_k\|^2$ by the descent
  lemma; rearranging gives (B).)

Group the two non-distance terms of the expected one-step bound and apply (A) to the cross
term:

$$
-2\eta_k\,\mathbf{g}_k^\top(\mathbf{x}_k-\mathbf{x}^\star) + \eta_k^2\|\mathbf{g}_k\|^2
\;\le\; -2\eta_k\Bigl(f(\mathbf{x}_k)-f^\star\Bigr) - \mu\eta_k\,D_k
   + \eta_k^2\|\mathbf{g}_k\|^2,
$$

where $D_k=\|\mathbf{x}_k-\mathbf{x}^\star\|^2$ and the $-\mu\eta_k D_k$ piece came from the
$\tfrac\mu2\|\cdot\|^2$ term in (A) (times the $-2\eta_k$ in front). The remaining job is to
show the two terms $-2\eta_k(f(\mathbf{x}_k)-f^\star)+\eta_k^2\|\mathbf{g}_k\|^2$ are
$\le 0$, so they can be dropped. Factor out $\eta_k$ and use (B) in the form
$\|\mathbf{g}_k\|^2\le 2L\bigl(f(\mathbf{x}_k)-f^\star\bigr)$:

$$
-2\eta_k(f(\mathbf{x}_k)-f^\star)+\eta_k^2\|\mathbf{g}_k\|^2
\;\le\; \eta_k\bigl(f(\mathbf{x}_k)-f^\star\bigr)\bigl(-2 + 2L\eta_k\bigr)
\;\le\; 0
\quad\text{whenever } \eta_k\le\tfrac1L,
$$

since $f(\mathbf{x}_k)-f^\star\ge 0$ and $-2+2L\eta_k\le 0$ exactly when $\eta_k\le 1/L$. So,
for every $k$ with $\eta_k\le 1/L$, these terms drop out and we are left with only the
contraction and the noise. Taking the full expectation $\mathbb{E}[\cdot]$ over all the
randomness through step $k$ (the per-step bound holds conditionally on $\mathbf{x}_k$, and the
tower property turns the conditional bound into one on the unconditional $D_k=\mathbb{E}
\|\mathbf{x}_k-\mathbf{x}^\star\|^2$):

$$
D_{k+1} \le (1 - \mu\eta_k)\,D_k + \eta_k^2\,\sigma^2.
$$ {#eq-recursion}

The recursion @eq-recursion is the heart of the matter: each step **contracts** the distance
by the factor $(1-\mu\eta_k)$ — the geometric shrinkage that gave GD its linear rate — but
then **adds back** a noise term $\eta_k^2\sigma^2$. The contraction alone wants $\rho^k$; the
additive noise destroys it. (The schedule below has $\eta_k\le 1/L$ once $k+1\ge 2L/\mu=2\kappa$;
the finitely many early steps where $\eta_k>1/L$ only shift the constant and are absorbed into
$C$ — that transient is exactly why the bound is $O(1/k)$, not equality.)

Solve the recursion with $\eta_k=\tfrac{2}{\mu(k+1)}$. We claim $D_k\le \tfrac{c}{k}$ for the
constant $c=\dfrac{4\sigma^2}{\mu^2}$ and prove it by induction on $k$. With this schedule the
contraction factor is
$$
1-\mu\eta_k = 1-\frac{2}{k+1}=\frac{k-1}{k+1},
\qquad\text{and the noise term is}\qquad
\eta_k^2\sigma^2=\frac{4\sigma^2}{\mu^2(k+1)^2}=\frac{c}{(k+1)^2}.
$$
Substitute the inductive hypothesis $D_k\le c/k$ into @eq-recursion (both coefficients are
nonnegative for $k\ge 1$, so the inequality is preserved):

$$
D_{k+1} \le \frac{k-1}{k+1}\cdot\frac{c}{k} + \frac{c}{(k+1)^2}
= c\left[\frac{k-1}{k(k+1)} + \frac{1}{(k+1)^2}\right].
$$

We want this $\le \tfrac{c}{k+1}$, i.e. (dividing by $c>0$ and multiplying through by
$(k+1)$) we need
$$
\frac{k-1}{k} + \frac{1}{k+1} \;\le\; 1.
$$
Check it directly: $\frac{k-1}{k}=1-\frac1k$, so the left side is $1-\frac1k+\frac1{k+1}$, and
$$
-\frac1k+\frac1{k+1} = \frac{-(k+1)+k}{k(k+1)} = \frac{-1}{k(k+1)} \;\le\; 0
\qquad\text{for all } k\ge 1.
$$
Hence the left side is $\le 1$, the desired inequality holds, and $D_{k+1}\le\tfrac{c}{k+1}$,
closing the induction. (The base case: for any starting $D_1$, enlarge $c$ to
$\max\{4\sigma^2/\mu^2,\,D_1\}$ if needed so $D_1\le c/1$ holds; this only changes the constant,
keeping $c=O(\sigma^2/\mu^2)$.) Thus $D_k\le c/k$ with $c=O(\sigma^2/\mu^2)$.

Finally, $L$-smoothness converts distance to function value: by the descent lemma at the
optimum (the same step (B) gives $f(\mathbf{x}_k)-f^\star\le\tfrac{L}{2}\|\mathbf{x}_k-
\mathbf{x}^\star\|^2$, since $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^\star)=\mathbf{0}$ kills the first-order
term in the smoothness upper bound around $\mathbf{x}^\star$). Taking expectations,
$\mathbb{E}[f(\mathbf{x}_k)]-f^\star\le\tfrac{L}{2}D_k\le\tfrac{Lc}{2k}$, which is @eq-sgd-rate
with $C=\tfrac{L}{2}c=O\!\bigl(L\sigma^2/\mu^2\bigr)$. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

This is the headline result, and the comparison to [OPTM-07] is the thing to carry away.

::: {.callout-important title="GD vs. SGD, side by side"}
On a $\mu$-strongly-convex, $L$-smooth objective:

| | per-step cost | rate | iterations to error $\varepsilon$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Full-batch GD** | $O(n)$ | linear: $(1-\tfrac\mu L)^k$ | $O\bigl(\kappa\log\tfrac1\varepsilon\bigr)$ |
| **SGD** | $O(1)$ | sublinear: $O(1/k)$ | $O\bigl(\tfrac{1}{\varepsilon}\bigr)$ |

GD's *rate* is vastly better (geometric vs. $1/k$): GD reaches $\varepsilon$ in a number of
*iterations* that grows only like $\log\tfrac1\varepsilon$, while SGD needs $\sim 1/\varepsilon$.
**But each GD iteration costs $n$ times as much.** Multiply rate by cost: to reach a *modest*
accuracy on a *huge* dataset, SGD's cheap steps win overwhelmingly, because GD's first step
alone costs an entire data pass. The crossover is exactly the article's thesis: SGD trades
asymptotic rate for per-step cost, and that trade is a winner precisely when $n$ is large and
you do not need many digits of accuracy — the defining regime of machine learning.
:::

For a merely **convex** (not strongly convex) smooth objective, the same machinery with the
$\eta_k\propto 1/\sqrt{k}$ schedule gives the slower

$$
\mathbb{E}\bigl[f(\bar{\mathbf{x}}_k)\bigr] - f^\star = O\!\Bigl(\frac{1}{\sqrt{k}}\Bigr),
$$ {#eq-convex-rate}

where $\bar{\mathbf{x}}_k$ is the *running average* of the iterates (averaging is what tames
the noise in the convex case). The $1/\sqrt{k}$ rate is the price of dropping strong
convexity, and — unlike GD's $O(1/k)$ convex rate — it is provably *optimal* for stochastic
first-order methods: no method that sees only noisy gradients can beat $1/\sqrt{k}$ in the
worst case. That optimality is why SGD, plus the accelerations of [OPTM-08] and [OPTM-12],
is where the field settled.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"If SGD's rate is worse than GD's, in what precise sense is SGD 'better'?"* Be careful to
separate **per-iteration** from **per-computation**. Per iteration, GD is strictly better
(geometric vs. sublinear). The win is **per gradient evaluation**: GD's single step costs
$n$ per-example gradients and makes one geometric contraction; in that same budget SGD takes
$n$ steps. For the *first* unit of accuracy on a large redundant dataset, those $n$ cheap
noisy steps blow GD's one precise step out of the water — SGD often reaches a usable model
before GD finishes its first epoch. The honest flip side, which a good interviewer will press
for: for *high* accuracy (many digits), GD's geometric rate eventually overtakes SGD's
$1/k$ crawl, and on *small* datasets where a full gradient is cheap, GD or L-BFGS is simply
better (the edge case of @sec-edge). The right answer names the regime: *SGD wins when $n$ is
large and modest accuracy suffices; full-batch methods win when $n$ is small or you need high
precision.*
:::

# Variance reduction: how the field beat the $O(1/k)$ plateau {#sec-vr}

The theorem above looks like a verdict: stochastic gradients force the $O(1/k)$ rate, strictly
worse than GD's geometric $\rho^k$, and that is the cost of cheap steps. For decades it *was*
the verdict. Then, around 2013, a family of **variance-reduction** methods (**SAG**, Le Roux–
Schmidt–Bach; **SVRG**, Johnson–Zhang; **SAGA**) showed the verdict was wrong *for finite
sums*: you can keep $O(1)$-per-step cost and *recover the linear rate of full-batch GD*. The
key realization is worth stating, because it reframes the whole article.

The $O(1/k)$ rate came entirely from the noise term $\eta_k^2\sigma^2$ in @eq-recursion, and
$\sigma^2$ was the variance of the *raw* per-example gradient — which stays large even at the
optimum (@sec-variance). But what if we could build a stochastic gradient estimator that is
*still unbiased* yet whose variance **shrinks to zero as the iterates approach the optimum**?
Then the noise term would die off on its own, the recursion would contract geometrically, and
the linear rate would return. That is exactly what variance reduction does, and the trick is a
**control variate**: subtract off a correlated, known-mean quantity to cancel most of the
noise.

The cleanest version is **SVRG (Stochastic Variance-Reduced Gradient)**. Periodically (say,
once per epoch) compute one *full* gradient $\nabla f(\tilde{\mathbf{x}})$ at a snapshot point
$\tilde{\mathbf{x}}$ — an expensive $O(n)$ operation, but rare. Then for the many cheap steps
in between, use the corrected estimator

$$
\mathbf{v}_k = \nabla f_{i_k}(\mathbf{x}_k) - \nabla f_{i_k}(\tilde{\mathbf{x}})
   + \nabla f(\tilde{\mathbf{x}}).
$$ {#eq-svrg}

Read it as: *take the usual stochastic gradient, but correct it using the same example's
gradient at the snapshot, anchored by the known full gradient there.* It is still unbiased —
taking $\mathbb{E}_{i_k}$ of @eq-svrg, the first term averages to $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$, the
second to $\nabla f(\tilde{\mathbf{x}})$, and these cancel against the last constant term,
leaving $\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{v}_k]=\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ exactly. But its *variance* is small
when both $\mathbf{x}_k$ and $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}$ are near the optimum: there the two
$\nabla f_{i_k}$ terms are evaluated at nearby points and nearly cancel example-by-example, so
the wild per-example fluctuation subtracts out and only the (tiny, near-optimal) true gradient
remains. The variance $\to 0$ as $\mathbf{x}_k\to\mathbf{x}^\star$, the noise term vanishes,
and SVRG converges **linearly**, like GD, at SGD-like per-step cost. @fig-vr shows the
recovered fast rate.

```{python}
#| label: fig-vr
#| fig-cap: "Variance reduction recovers the linear rate. On a strongly-convex finite-sum (ridge-regularized least squares), plain SGD with a decaying step (red) crawls at the $O(1/k)$ rate of @thm-sgd-rate, while **SVRG** (green) -- using the corrected estimator @eq-svrg with a full-gradient snapshot each epoch -- converges *linearly* (a straight line on the log scale), matching full-batch GD's rate at a fraction of its cost. The control variate cancels the gradient noise as the iterates approach the optimum, killing the noise floor that throttled SGD."
rng = np.random.default_rng(1)
n, p = 400, 15
B = rng.standard_normal((n, p)); xtrue = rng.standard_normal(p)
t = B @ xtrue + 0.5 * rng.standard_normal(n)
lam = 0.5
def ffull(x): return 0.5 * np.mean((B @ x - t) ** 2) + 0.5 * lam * x @ x
def gi(x, i): return (B[i] @ x - t[i]) * B[i] + lam * x
def gfull(x): return B.T @ (B @ x - t) / n + lam * x
xstar = np.linalg.solve(B.T @ B / n + lam * np.eye(p), B.T @ t / n)
fstar = ffull(xstar)

# Plain SGD, decaying step
x = np.zeros(p); sgd_hist = [(0, ffull(x) - fstar)]
for k in range(1, 8000):
    i = rng.integers(n)
    x = x - (0.4 / (k + 100)) * gi(x, i)
    if k % 40 == 0: sgd_hist.append((k, ffull(x) - fstar))
sgd_hist = np.array(sgd_hist)

# SVRG
m = n            # inner loop length = one epoch
eta = 0.02
xt = np.zeros(p)
svrg_hist = [(0, ffull(xt) - fstar)]; grads_used = 0
for epoch in range(18):
    mu_full = gfull(xt); grads_used += n     # snapshot full gradient
    x = xt.copy()
    for k in range(m):
        i = rng.integers(n)
        v = gi(x, i) - gi(xt, i) + mu_full
        x = x - eta * v
        grads_used += 2
        if k % 40 == 0:
            svrg_hist.append((grads_used / n, ffull(x) - fstar))
    xt = x.copy()
svrg_hist = np.array(svrg_hist)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.8, 4.2))
ax.semilogy(sgd_hist[:, 0] / n, np.clip(sgd_hist[:, 1], 1e-13, None),
            color=STEP, label="plain SGD (decaying step)")
ax.semilogy(svrg_hist[:, 0], np.clip(svrg_hist[:, 1], 1e-13, None),
            color=ACCENT, lw=2.3, label="SVRG (variance-reduced)")
ax.set_xlabel("effective data passes (epochs)")
ax.set_ylabel(r"$f(\mathbf{x}_k)-f^\star$ (log scale)")
ax.set_title("Variance reduction restores GD's linear rate")
ax.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

A important honesty note: variance reduction's clean linear rate is a **finite-sum** result —
it exploits that there are exactly $n$ fixed examples whose gradients can be cached or
re-evaluated at a snapshot. In the **streaming / truly online** setting (infinite or
ever-arriving data), there is no fixed sum to anchor against, $\sigma^2$ genuinely never
vanishes, and the $O(1/\sqrt k)$ / $O(1/k)$ rates are the best possible. SVRG and friends are
also less dominant in deep learning than they are in convex finite-sum problems, because the
snapshot full gradient is expensive for huge models and the non-convex landscape muddies the
clean theory — there, plain SGD with momentum and adaptive scaling ([OPTM-08], [OPTM-12])
remains the workhorse. Variance reduction is the right lens for *why* SGD is slow (the noise
floor) and a genuine fix where the finite-sum structure can be exploited.

# The non-convex reality: saddles, and why noise helps {#sec-nonconvex}

Everything proven so far assumed convexity. The objectives SGD is most famously used for —
deep neural networks — are emphatically **non-convex**, and here the story inverts in a way
worth understanding, because it connects directly to [OPTM-02]'s saddle-point discussion.

[OPTM-02] showed that in high dimensions, the stationary points of a non-convex objective are
overwhelmingly **saddle points** (some directions curve up, others down), not local minima:
for a point to be a local min, *every* one of the $p$ Hessian eigenvalues must be positive,
and by chance that gets exponentially unlikely as $p$ grows. Saddles are the real obstacle to
optimization in high dimensions — a *deterministic* method like full-batch GD, landing exactly
on a saddle, has zero gradient and *stops*, stuck at a point that is not even a local minimum.

Here the noise that cost us convergence accuracy pays a dividend. **SGD's gradient noise kicks
the iterate off saddle points.** At a saddle the true gradient is zero, so GD freezes; but the
stochastic gradient $\nabla f_{i_k}$ is *not* zero there (the non-vanishing noise of
@sec-variance), so SGD gets a random shove, and with overwhelming probability that shove has a
component along a *downward* curving direction, sending the iterate off the saddle and back
into descent. The very property that prevents SGD from settling cleanly into a minimum is what
lets it *escape* the saddles that trap deterministic methods. This is a major reason SGD, not
full-batch GD, is the default for training deep networks — the noise is a feature, providing a
cheap, built-in mechanism for escaping the high-dimensional saddle landscape.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why train neural networks with SGD rather than a more accurate full-batch optimizer like
L-BFGS?"* Three reasons, and a strong answer hits all three. **(1) Cost:** a full gradient over
millions of examples per step is unaffordable; SGD's $O(1)$ steps are the only thing that fits
the compute budget. **(2) Saddle escape:** the gradient noise (@sec-nonconvex) shoves the
iterate off the saddle points that pervade high-dimensional non-convex losses and would freeze
a deterministic method. **(3) Generalization:** there is strong empirical (and growing
theoretical) evidence that SGD's noise acts as an *implicit regularizer*, biasing training
toward "flat" minima that generalize better to unseen data than the sharp minima a full-batch
method might find. So SGD is preferred not despite its noise but partly *because* of it — a
genuinely counterintuitive point that separates someone who has only read the convex theory
from someone who understands why the field actually trains the way it does.
:::

# The edge of applicability: when SGD is the wrong tool {#sec-edge}

An honest treatment must say where SGD *loses*, and it loses in exactly the regime opposite to
the one it was built for. The whole case for SGD rested on two things: $n$ is **large** (so a
full gradient is unaffordable) and we want **modest** accuracy (so the $O(1/k)$ crawl is fine).
Negate either and the calculus flips.

- **Small or medium $n$.** If the full gradient is cheap to compute — $n$ in the thousands, or
  the data fit comfortably in memory — there is little reason to settle for noisy steps. A
  full-batch method enjoys the *geometric* rate (and Newton/L-BFGS the *quadratic* rate near
  the optimum, [OPTM-09]), reaching machine precision in a handful of clean iterations where
  SGD would still be jittering around its floor. *Rule of thumb: if you can afford the full
  gradient and want a precise answer, use a full-batch method.*

- **High accuracy required.** SGD's $O(1/k)$ rate means each additional digit of accuracy
  costs *ten times* more iterations (and a constant step never gets below its floor at all).
  When you need the optimum to many digits — some scientific computing, some statistical
  estimation where the *exact* MLE matters — SGD is the wrong tool; switch to a full-batch
  method, or use SGD to get close fast and then *polish* with a few full-batch / Newton steps.

- **Severe ill-conditioning.** SGD inherits GD's sensitivity to the condition number $\kappa$
  ([OPTM-07]) *and* adds noise on top, so a badly stretched, ill-conditioned bowl is doubly
  punishing: the iterates zig-zag (large $\kappa$) *and* jitter (variance). Here second-order
  methods that rescale away $\kappa$ ([OPTM-09]) or adaptive per-coordinate methods
  ([OPTM-12]) are far more effective than plain SGD.

@fig-edge makes the small-$n$ case concrete: on a small, well-conditioned least-squares
problem, L-BFGS reaches the optimum in a handful of iterations while SGD is still bouncing
around its noise floor — the honest boundary where the article's whole thesis runs out.

```{python}
#| label: fig-edge
#| fig-cap: "The edge of applicability. A *small* ($n=300$), moderately conditioned least-squares problem -- the regime where SGD is the *wrong* choice. **Full-batch GD** (navy) converges geometrically; **L-BFGS** (a quasi-Newton method, [OPTM-09]) reaches near machine precision in a dozen iterations. **SGD** (red), even with a decaying step, crawls at $O(1/k)$ and is still far from the optimum after thousands of cheap steps. When the full gradient is affordable and you want a precise answer, the cheap-noisy-step trade-off SGD is built on simply does not pay off."
from scipy.optimize import minimize
rng = np.random.default_rng(2)
n, p = 300, 20
B = rng.standard_normal((n, p)); xtrue = rng.standard_normal(p)
t = B @ xtrue + 0.3 * rng.standard_normal(n)
def ffull(x): return 0.5 * np.mean((B @ x - t) ** 2)
def gfull(x): return B.T @ (B @ x - t) / n
def gi(x, i): return (B[i] @ x - t[i]) * B[i]
xstar = np.linalg.solve(B.T @ B, B.T @ t); fstar = ffull(xstar)

# Full-batch GD
L = np.linalg.eigvalsh(B.T @ B / n).max()
x = np.zeros(p); gd_hist = [ffull(x) - fstar]
for _ in range(120):
    x = x - (1.0 / L) * gfull(x); gd_hist.append(ffull(x) - fstar)

# L-BFGS, recording the objective each iteration
lbfgs_hist = []
def cb(xk): lbfgs_hist.append(ffull(xk) - fstar)
minimize(ffull, np.zeros(p), jac=gfull, method="L-BFGS-B", callback=cb,
         options={"maxiter": 60, "gtol": 1e-14, "ftol": 1e-16})

# SGD, decaying step
x = np.zeros(p); sgd_hist = []
for k in range(1, 8000):
    i = rng.integers(n)
    x = x - (0.05 / (1 + k / 200)) * gi(x, i)
    if k % 40 == 0: sgd_hist.append(ffull(x) - fstar)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.8, 4.2))
ax.semilogy(np.arange(len(gd_hist)), np.clip(gd_hist, 1e-16, None),
            color=CURVE, label="full-batch GD")
ax.semilogy(np.arange(1, len(lbfgs_hist) + 1), np.clip(lbfgs_hist, 1e-16, None),
            color=ACCENT, lw=2.3, label="L-BFGS (quasi-Newton)")
ax.semilogy(np.linspace(0, len(gd_hist), len(sgd_hist)),
            np.clip(sgd_hist, 1e-16, None), color=STEP, label="SGD (decaying step)")
ax.set_xlabel("iterations (full-batch scale)")
ax.set_ylabel(r"$f-f^\star$ (log scale)")
ax.set_title("Small $n$: full-batch methods crush SGD")
ax.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from **[OPTM-06 *Gradient Descent*]** (the update rule and steepest descent) and
**[OPTM-07 *Step Size and Convergence*]** (the descent lemma, $L$-smoothness, strong
convexity, the condition number, and the clean GD rates). This article kept the update almost
unchanged but replaced the *exact* gradient with an **unbiased noisy estimate** — one example
or a minibatch — and re-derived the whole convergence story under that noise: the **noise
floor** of a constant step, the **Robbins–Monro** decaying-step conditions, the **minibatch**
variance/cost trade-off, the slower **$O(1/k)$ / $O(1/\sqrt k)$** rates, and the
**variance-reduction** fix. From here:

- **[OPTM-12 *Adaptive and Modern Optimizers*]** — AdaGrad, RMSProp, and **Adam** are all
  refinements of the bare SGD step @eq-sgd: per-coordinate step sizes (to handle the
  ill-conditioning of @sec-edge) and momentum ([OPTM-08]) layered on top of the stochastic
  gradient. This is the next article, and it assumes everything here.
- **[OPTM-08 *Momentum*]** — momentum and SGD combine into the *SGD-with-momentum* that trains
  most networks; the zig-zag damping there and the noise averaging here are complementary.

It is also the **training engine** the rest of the series links back to: **Classification**
and **Unsupervised Learning** invoke "fit by SGD" for logistic regression, neural nets, and
large-scale models, and every deep-model treatment rests on the cheap-noisy-step trade-off
derived here.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **The founding paper.** H. Robbins & S. Monro, "A Stochastic Approximation Method," *Annals
  of Mathematical Statistics* 22 (1951) — the origin of the decaying-step conditions @eq-rm1–
  @eq-rm2 and of the whole field of stochastic approximation. Remarkably readable.
- **The modern convergence theory.** Bottou, Curtis & Nocedal, "Optimization Methods for
  Large-Scale Machine Learning," *SIAM Review* 60 (2018) — the definitive survey of SGD for ML,
  including the noise-floor / decaying-step analysis at exactly this article's level. Nesterov's
  *Lectures on Convex Optimization* (2018) gives the optimal-rate lower bounds that make
  $O(1/\sqrt k)$ provably best for stochastic first-order methods.
- **Variance reduction.** Johnson & Zhang, "Accelerating Stochastic Gradient Descent using
  Predictive Variance Reduction" (SVRG, 2013); Defazio, Bach & Lacoste-Julien (SAGA, 2014);
  Le Roux, Schmidt & Bach (SAG, 2012) — the papers that recovered the linear rate for finite
  sums via the control-variate idea of @sec-vr.
- **Open threads this article deliberately deferred.** *How do we choose a per-coordinate or
  adaptive step instead of one scalar $\eta_k$?* — AdaGrad/RMSProp/Adam, [OPTM-12]. *Does the
  noise help non-convex training, and how?* — the saddle-escape and implicit-regularization
  story of @sec-nonconvex, an active research area. *What if part of the objective is a
  non-smooth penalty (lasso)?* — proximal *stochastic* gradient, building on [OPTM-16].
